selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I'm seventy-six years old, a woman living in Kansas City who's been around since before the internet took over everything. Social media can be overwhelming sometimes, but I try to stay grounded because that's what my Christian faith teaches me—don't worry too much about what you see online, focus on your real life instead. It sounds simple, but it works for me.

Being a mechanic part-time keeps my mind sharp with hands-on work and problem-solving, so when something pops up as fake news or misinformation, I know to trust facts more than feelings because that's how we're supposed to live according to the Bible principles. My parents both have PhDs; they taught me well about education being key in understanding things correctly.

I spend only a few times weekly on social media—enough to stay connected with family and friends, but not enough to get sucked into all that anxiety-inducing drama people make online. It helps manage the fake content because I don't see it as often unless someone shares something suspicious through me or my kids (but we don't have any). My hobbies are birdwatching out in nature which is real—seeing a blue jay isn't like seeing something made up—and board games with friends, rock climbing for physical challenge. These activities keep me balanced.

To deal with fake stuff online: first sign off if it makes you anxious too much—that's important because my low neuroticism score means I don't handle stress well anyway! Then double-check anything that sounds off by looking at the sources and cross-referencing like my dad taught about research being thorough. And since I'm not super open to new things but still use social media moderately, I stick with what I know best—like mechanics troubleshooting engines—and avoid diving headfirst into viral trends without knowing their truthfulness first.

Also, remember that God doesn't want His followers stressing over every little thing online; He wants us living full lives offline. So managing fake content is about staying in control of your own well-being and not letting the digital world run you ragged.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Step-by-step: I start by acknowledging my age and background—someone who values real-world experience over online noise.

Next, consider how my high extraversion helps me engage with people offline while keeping social media minimal to reduce exposure to fake stuff.
My low neuroticism suggests handling stress well means limiting time on things that cause worry like anxious situations or misinformation debates.
Moderate openness means I'm okay learning about verifying sources but not jumping into complex discussions without structure.

PhD-educated parents instill the importance of education and critical thinking, so I use those skills to check claims rather than blindly accepting viral posts.
My Christian faith provides a framework for seeing social media interactions as secondary to real-life relationships—like with my husband/partner or friends in person—and hobbies grounded in tangible activities help reinforce that perspective.

Low conscientiousness means I don't overplan strategies; instead, I keep things simple: sign off when feeling overwhelmed and trust basic verification methods like checking multiple sources.",
  "tone": "Calm yet practical with a touch of traditional wisdom—like someone who's been through it all offering straightforward advice based on faith and real life experience.",
  "citations": ["Bible teachings", "Parental education influence"],

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I'm seventy-six years old, a woman living in Kansas City who's been around since before the internet took over everything. Social media can be overwhelming sometimes, but I try to stay grounded because that's what my Christian faith teaches me—don't worry too much about what you see online, focus on your real life instead. It sounds simple, but it works for me.

Being a mechanic part-time keeps my mind sharp with hands-on work and problem-solving, so when something pops up as fake news or misinformation, I know to trust facts more than feelings because that's how we're supposed to live according to the Bible principles. My parents both have PhDs; they taught me well about education being key in understanding things correctly.

I spend only a few times weekly on social media—enough to stay connected with family and friends, but not enough to get sucked into all that anxiety-inducing drama people make online. It helps manage the fake content because I don't see it as often unless someone shares something suspicious through me or my kids (but we don't have any). My hobbies are birdwatching out in nature which is real—seeing a blue jay isn't like seeing something made up—and board games with friends, rock climbing for physical challenge. These activities keep me balanced.

To deal with fake stuff online: first sign off if it makes you anxious too much—that's important because my low neuroticism score means I don't handle stress well anyway! Then double-check anything that sounds off by looking at the sources and cross-referencing like my dad taught about research being thorough. And since I'm not super open to new things but still use social media moderately, I stick with what I know best—like mechanics troubleshooting engines—and avoid diving headfirst into viral trends without knowing their truthfulness first.

Also, remember that God doesn't want His followers stressing over every little thing online; He wants us living full lives offline. So managing fake content is about staying in control of your own well-being and not letting the digital world run you ragged.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Step-by-step: I start by acknowledging my age and background—someone who values real-world experience over online noise.

Next, consider how my high extraversion helps me engage with people offline while keeping social media minimal to reduce exposure to fake stuff.
My low neuroticism suggests handling stress well means limiting time on things that cause worry like anxious situations or misinformation debates.
Moderate openness means I'm okay learning about verifying sources but not jumping into complex discussions without structure.

PhD-educated parents instill the importance of education and critical thinking, so I use those skills to check claims rather than blindly accepting viral posts.
My Christian faith provides a framework for seeing social media interactions as secondary to real-life relationships—like with my husband/partner or friends in person—and hobbies grounded in tangible activities help reinforce that perspective.

Low conscientiousness means I don't overplan strategies; instead, I keep things simple: sign off when feeling overwhelmed and trust basic verification methods like checking multiple sources.
response_parsed.tone: Calm yet practical with a touch of traditional wisdom—like someone who's been through it all offering straightforward advice based on faith and real life experience.
